original_abstract: This article examines manifest inter-party conflict over policy issues and the capacity of coalition agreements to resolve these disputes. Drawing on empirical evidence from Germany, Belgium, Italy, and the Netherlands, we analyze the characteristics of these agreements—including specific deals struck over controversial topics—and track the conflicts that emerged during the lifetimes of eleven different governments. Specifically, we investigate how often parties in power are constrained by written records of past negotiations. Our findings reveal that coalition agreements in all four countries contain substantial policy mandates, with roughly one-third of these deals being precisely defined. These agreements do not merely reflect easy consensus; they frequently tackle highly contentious issues. Our central finding is that when intra-coalition conflict occurs, parties almost invariably return to the original policy deals if they were documented at the start of the term. Consequently, rather than being mere "cheap talk," coalition agreements serve as vital blueprints that facilitate collective decision-making and stabilize governance across diverse political systems.
humanized_abstract: In this paper we focus on manifest interparty conflict concerning policy issues and the potential role of coalition agreements in resolving them. Based on information about policy issues in coalition agreements from Germany, Belgium, Italy and the Netherlands, and on data about the extent of intracoalition conflict during the lives of eleven different West European coalition governments, we investigate what characterises policy mandating in such agreements (i.e. what kind of policy issues are included, and what form of agreements concerning them are produced) and how frequently in the course of a term coalition parties must contend with each other on the issues included. Our empirical analyses provide the following information: (a) all four types of Western European democracy have policy mandating in the form of coalition agreements containing considerable detail of policy content, which is, however, specified with exactness in only about one third of the cases included in our analyses; and (b) in each of the four cases, in almost all instances where intracoalition conflicts over policy issues were registered during the lifetime of a particular coalition, the relevant policy provisions from the parties' coalition agreement had to be referred to in the course of settling the conflicts. In short, all four examples of West European democracies show that policy mandating by means of formal coalition agreements may induce parties in a governing coalition to refrain from manifest conflict in the course of a consequence that makes the nature of policy mandating in such agreements anything but that of 'cheap talk'.
